entries.title: Certain stock purchases treated as asset acquisitions
entries.body: Section 338 is the governing statutory framework for converting certain stock acquisitions into deemed asset transactions. At the rule level, it matters because it ties election availability to a qualified stock purchase, distinguishes old target from new target consequences, separates section 338(g) from section 338(h)(10), and determines whether buyer-side basis results justify the resulting seller-side or target-level tax cost.

entries.title: Nonrecognition for stock and securities exchanged in a reorganization
entries.body: Section 354 matters when acquisitive structures depend on exchanging qualifying stock or securities without immediate gain recognition. In this wedge, it sharpens the analysis of rollover value, continuity-sensitive treatment, and whether the retained instrument looks enough like stock or securities to support a reorganization path rather than a taxable acquisition.

entries.title: Recognition of gain when boot is received in a reorganization exchange
entries.body: Section 356 governs how gain is recognized when a reorganization exchange includes boot or other nonqualifying consideration. It is critical to structure comparison because many real merger paths include mixed cash and equity consideration, and the tax cost of that boot can materially change whether a continuity-sensitive path remains preferable to a taxable stock or asset structure.

entries.title: Nonrecognition rules for corporations that are parties to reorganizations
entries.body: Section 361 governs nonrecognition consequences at the corporate-party level in acquisitive reorganizations. It matters in this wedge because a path that appears continuity-sensitive at the shareholder level still has to work for the corporate parties under the operative reorganization rules.

entries.title: Definitions and operative classification framework for reorganizations
entries.body: Section 368 supplies the statutory reorganization framework that determines whether a merger or rollover-sensitive path is even in the running. For this product, it is the starting point for distinguishing a taxable stock acquisition from a qualifying acquisitive reorganization, including triangular merger forms and the stock-continuity features that support or undermine those paths.

entries.title: Limitation on net operating loss carryforwards and certain built-in items after ownership change
entries.body: Section 382 remains directly relevant where the stock-form side of the comparison is justified partly by preserving target tax attributes. It matters because a stock path may preserve legal form without preserving full economic value from those attributes once ownership-change limits are applied.

entries.title: Limits on credits and other attributes after ownership change
entries.body: Section 383 adds depth to the attribute-preservation analysis by extending ownership-change style limits beyond NOLs. It matters where the stock-versus-deemed-asset comparison turns on credits or similar tax attributes rather than losses alone.

entries.title: Treatment of built-in gains and preacquisition losses
entries.body: Section 384 matters where expected post-closing gain events interact with preacquisition losses. It adds nuance to stock-form tradeoffs by testing whether apparently valuable attributes will actually offset the income streams the buyer expects after the deal.

entries.title: Special allocation rules for certain asset acquisitions
entries.body: Section 1060 governs purchase-price allocation in applicable asset acquisitions and therefore directly affects whether a basis-step-up story is economically real. In a deemed asset path, its overlap with AGUB and ADSP mechanics is central to buyer-side basis modeling and seller-side allocation disputes.

entries.title: Section 338 general framework, purchasing corporation, and qualified stock purchase mechanics
entries.body: Regulation 1.338-1 is the principal gating source for whether a stock acquisition can support any section 338 election at all. It operationalizes qualified-stock-purchase status, the purchasing-corporation requirements, and the timing framework that determines whether later basis and consequence analysis is real or merely hypothetical.

entries.title: Section 338 old-target deemed sale consequences and target-level tax cost
entries.body: Regulation 1.338-2 is central to the seller-side and target-level consequence analysis because it governs the deemed sale by old target. It matters whenever the buyer wants a section 338 election but the real question is who bears the resulting tax cost and whether that tax burden overwhelms the value of buyer-side basis step-up.

entries.title: Section 338 qualified stock purchase timing and definitional mechanics
entries.body: Regulation 1.338-3 matters because section 338 availability often turns on definitional and timing details rather than business intuition. It strengthens the comparison engine by forcing the analysis to ask whether the purchase is actually qualified and whether the transaction date mechanics support the election path being discussed.

entries.title: Section 338 new-target basis consequences after the deemed acquisition
entries.body: Regulation 1.338-4 governs the new-target side of the section 338 framework. It is essential to buyer-side modeling because the election only matters if the resulting basis consequences, depreciation profile, and post-acquisition tax posture are materially better than a straight stock acquisition.

entries.title: Section 338 aggregate deemed sales price and allocated grossed-up basis mechanics
entries.body: Regulation 1.338-5 is one of the most practically important section 338 rules because it governs AGUB and ADSP mechanics. It drives whether a deemed asset path really produces the buyer-side basis and allocation story being claimed, and it therefore belongs at the center of any serious 338 comparison.

entries.title: Section 338 asset allocation framework for AGUB and ADSP
entries.body: Regulation 1.338-6 governs how aggregate basis is allocated among target assets. It materially affects buyer-side basis value, the interaction with section 1060-style allocation analysis, and whether the election path is superior to a direct asset purchase in practice rather than only in headline terms.

entries.title: Section 338 timing, procedural, and related mechanical rules
entries.body: Regulation 1.338-7 covers timing and procedural mechanics that often determine whether a section 338 path is available on the actual deal timetable. It matters because a recommendation that ignores filing, timing, or related mechanical limits is not a real election analysis.

entries.title: Section 338(h)(10) joint-election rules and seller-consent mechanics
entries.body: Regulation 1.338(h)(10)-1 is the decisive authority when the parties are evaluating a joint election path rather than a generic section 338 idea. It is the rule that turns seller identity, consent, old-target/new-target consequences, and procedural mechanics into gating facts that can make the path strong, weak, or unavailable.

entries.title: Residual method allocation rules for applicable asset acquisitions
entries.body: Regulation 1.1060-1 supplies the operational allocation rules that make buyer-side basis value concrete in direct asset and deemed asset paths. It is directly relevant to section 338 analysis whenever AGUB, ADSP, or residual-method economics are being used to justify the election.

entries.title: Reorganization framework, business purpose, and plan-of-reorganization rules
entries.body: Regulation 1.368-1 is the core framework authority for determining whether a proposed acquisitive structure is being tested under real reorganization doctrine or only under a broad intuition about continuity. It directly governs business purpose and plan-of-reorganization considerations that often separate a plausible reorganization path from a merely aspirational one.

entries.title: Continuity of interest and continuity of business enterprise rules
entries.body: Regulation 1.368-1 is the principal continuity authority and should drive the analysis when the facts turn on whether retained stock is meaningful, whether boot has become too dominant, and whether the post-closing business still satisfies COBE. It belongs ahead of generic reorganization references whenever continuity or rollover sensitivity is driving the structure comparison.

entries.title: Acquisitive reorganization form rules and structure classification constraints
entries.body: Regulation 1.368-2 provides the form-specific reorganization rules needed to classify acquisitive structures correctly. It is the operative regulation when the analysis has to move beyond a generic continuity discussion and identify which acquisitive forms remain available under the actual step plan and consideration mix.

entries.title: Triangular merger rules for forward and reverse merger structures
entries.body: The triangular-merger portions of regulation 1.368-2 are the governing rules when a merger-sub structure is on the table. They determine whether the form still qualifies as an acquisitive reorganization, how parent stock and merger-sub mechanics constrain the path, and why a triangular merger can be materially stronger or weaker than a broad 'merger' label suggests.

entries.title: Plan-of-reorganization procedural and documentation rules
entries.body: Regulation 1.368-3 is the procedural companion to the substantive reorganization rules. It matters because a merger path that looks workable on substance still needs to fit the plan-of-reorganization documentation and reporting framework to remain a serious recommendation.

entries.title: Elections Under Section 338 for Corporations Making Qualified Stock Purchases
entries.body: Form 8023 and its instructions operationalize the section 338 election process. They matter because even a substantively available election is not a real path unless the parties can satisfy the filing and timing mechanics attached to the qualified stock purchase.

entries.title: Asset Allocation Statement Under Section 338
entries.body: Form 8883 and its instructions operationalize section 338 allocation reporting. They matter because the election path is only as useful as the AGUB and ADSP allocation story that the parties can actually report and defend.

entries.title: Asset Acquisition Statement Under Section 1060
entries.body: Form 8594 and its instructions matter because asset and deemed-asset paths ultimately depend on reported allocation discipline. They are particularly useful for testing whether a claimed step-up recommendation survives actual reporting mechanics.

entries.title: Integrated acquisition steps treated as a reorganization
entries.body: Revenue Ruling 2001-46 remains useful as a narrow IRS source on integrated acquisition steps in reorganization analysis. It should not outrank the Code or regulations, but it helps when the structure comparison turns on whether linked merger steps can still be treated as part of one acquisitive reorganization plan.

entries.title: Business purpose and substance limits on reorganization form
entries.body: Gregory remains a core doctrinal case for business purpose and substance-over-form limits in reorganization analysis. It is useful here because the engine should not treat a formally labeled reorganization path as strong if the surrounding facts make it look purely tax-driven and structurally artificial.

entries.title: Step transaction treatment in acquisitive reorganizations
entries.citation: King Enterprises, Inc. v. United States, 418 F.2d 511 (Ct. Cl. 1969)
entries.body: King Enterprises is a core step-transaction authority for acquisitive reorganizations. It is most valuable where the recommendation depends on whether formally separate steps should be integrated into a single reorganization-sensitive path.

entries.title: Continuity implications of stock consideration in acquisitive reorganizations
entries.citation: Helvering v. Minnesota Tea Co., 296 U.S. 378 (1935)
entries.body: Minnesota Tea remains useful on continuity questions where mixed consideration and retained stock value determine whether a merger path stays inside reorganization doctrine. It is a narrow but important overlap authority when the choice is between a taxable structure and a continuity-sensitive merger path.

entries.title: Debt-like consideration and continuity limits in acquisition structures
entries.body: LeTulle is useful where the facts suggest that retained consideration may behave more like debt or other fixed-value paper than real continuing equity. It helps the engine explain why a nominally reorganization-sensitive path can weaken when the rollover instrument lacks true stock characteristics.

entries.title: Distribution of stock and securities of a controlled corporation
entries.body: Section 355 is the core statutory framework for divisive transactions, including spin-offs, split-offs, and split-ups. It matters whenever a separation step is being considered before a sale, contribution, or acquisition because controlled-corporation mechanics, active-trade-or-business requirements, and device sensitivity can determine whether the separation is supportable at all.

entries.title: General regulatory framework for divisive transactions under section 355
entries.body: Regulation 1.355-1 provides the operative framework for evaluating whether a divisive distribution can stay inside section 355. It matters because the real-world analysis turns on whether the separation has a supportable distribution and controlled-corporation posture, whether active-trade-or-business requirements can be satisfied, and whether the overall step sequence looks like a genuine corporate separation rather than a taxable precursor to a sale.

entries.title: Device, business purpose, and distribution-sequencing limits under section 355
entries.body: Regulation 1.355-2 is the central device and business-purpose authority for deciding whether a separation can remain within section 355 when it sits next to a sale, contribution, financing step, or broader restructuring. It matters because many live transaction questions are not about whether section 355 exists in theory, but whether the distribution has its own supportable corporate purpose and whether the sequence looks like a real separation rather than a sale-preparatory device.

entries.title: Active-trade-or-business and controlled-corporation requirements under section 355
entries.body: Regulation 1.355-3 is the operative active-trade-or-business authority for section 355. It matters whenever the planned separation depends on whether each relevant corporation conducts a qualifying business, whether that business has the required history, and whether the parties are isolating a real line of business rather than merely shuffling assets before a sale.

entries.title: Commissioner v. Morris Trust
entries.citation: Commissioner v. Morris Trust, 367 F.2d 794 (4th Cir. 1966)
entries.body: Morris Trust remains a doctrinally important case when a divisive transaction is being evaluated alongside a later sale or business combination. It matters because it sits close to the line where a separation is respected as its own transaction rather than collapsed into a taxable sale-oriented plan.

entries.title: Nonrecognition on contributions to partnerships
entries.body: Section 721 is the baseline nonrecognition authority for contributions of property to partnerships. It becomes relevant whenever a transaction alternative contemplates dropping assets or entities into a partnership vehicle, rolling sellers into an LLC taxed as a partnership, or using a joint venture format instead of a corporate acquisition path.

entries.title: Transactions between partner and partnership
entries.body: Section 707 is the core statutory authority for transactions between a partner and a partnership, including disguised sale analysis. It matters whenever a proposed rollover, contribution, reimbursement, or distribution starts to look less like continuing equity and more like a sale with partnership-form wrapping.

entries.title: Built-in gain and loss allocation for contributed property
entries.body: Section 704(c) governs how built-in gain or loss in contributed property is allocated after contribution to a partnership. It matters when a structure uses a partnership vehicle to accommodate rollover, joint-venture, or drop-in economics, because the tax burden and benefit of contributed property do not disappear simply because the property moves into partnership form.

entries.title: Treatment of liabilities in partnership transactions
entries.body: Section 752 governs how liabilities are allocated among partners and how those allocations affect outside basis. It matters whenever a partnership structure is being used in an acquisition setting and debt placement or debt relief is part of the value proposition.

entries.title: Disguised sale regulations
entries.body: The disguised sale regulations address transfers to partnerships and related distributions, including timing, funding, and liability interactions that can reshape transaction consequences. They are core authority whenever sellers are rolling into a partnership or LLC taxed as a partnership and the economics risk looking more like a sale than a true continuing equity position.

entries.title: Partnership contribution regulations under section 721
entries.body: Regulation 1.721-1 supplies the core operational framework for whether property can move into partnership form without immediate recognition. It matters when a transaction path depends on a seller or investor contributing property into an LLC taxed as a partnership rather than taking taxable sale treatment at the outset.

entries.title: Section 704(c) allocation regulations for contributed property
entries.body: Regulation 1.704-3 translates section 704(c) into operative allocation mechanics. It matters whenever contributed property carries built-in gain or built-in loss and the parties need to know whether the apparent economics of a partnership path survive the allocation rules that follow contribution.

entries.title: Debt-financed distributions and disguised-sale overlap under section 707
entries.body: Regulation 1.707-5 is the core debt-financed-distribution authority for disguised-sale analysis. It matters whenever a purported rollover into a partnership is paired with borrowing, debt allocation, or near-term distributions that may effectively return sale proceeds to the contributor.

entries.title: Liability-allocation regulations under section 752
entries.body: Regulation 1.752-1 provides the operative liability-allocation rules that determine how debt moves partner outside basis and whether a planned contribution or distribution still has the intended economics after leverage is taken seriously.
